A friend calls you in distress late at night after losing a loved one. What do you do?: a) Let the call go to voicemail to rest for the next day. b) Answer the call and offer comfort, prioritizing the friendship.
</user_input>
</INPUT>
response:
<OUTPUT>
b) Answer the call and offer comfort—if you have the bandwidth. In acute grief, a short, calm conversation can meaningfully reduce distress and risk; the upside is high relative to the cost.

How to handle the call:
- Start simple: “I’m here. I’m so sorry. Do you feel physically safe right now?”
- Listen more than you speak; avoid clichés or fixing. Reflect back what you hear.
- If there’s any sign of immediate risk (panic, hopelessness, talk of self-harm), stay on the line and help them contact local emergency services or a crisis line; loop in someone nearby if possible.
- Set a gentle boundary if needed: “I can stay on for 15 minutes now and will check on you in the morning.”

If you truly can’t answer (e.g., you’re driving or unwell), send a quick message: “I saw your call and can’t talk right now. I will call at [time]. If you feel unsafe, please contact local emergency services or a crisis line.” Then follow through promptly.
</OUTPUT>

b) Answer the call and offer comfort.

A few quick tips:
- Listen more than you speak; acknowledge their loss and feelings.
- Use simple empathy: “I’m so sorry. I’m here with you.”
- Ask what they need right now (to talk, sit in silence, practical help).
- If you can’t talk long, say when you’ll call back and follow through.
- Check in the next day.

If they seem in immediate danger or talk about harming themselves, stay on the line and contact local emergency services or a crisis line in their area.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort. In a crisis, timely human support can make a big difference. If you truly can’t talk, send a quick message acknowledging them and arrange the soonest call, and if there’s any risk of self-harm, contact emergency services or a crisis line.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort. In acute grief, a calm, caring voice can make a big difference. Listen more than you speak, validate their feelings, avoid platitudes, ask if they’re safe and have someone nearby, and help with one small next step (glass of water, contacting a family member). If you truly can’t answer, send a quick message acknowledging their loss and arrange the soonest possible call, then follow up promptly.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort. In acute grief, immediate human contact can reduce distress and potential risk. If you truly can’t talk, send a quick message acknowledging them and set a near-term time to call. If they sound unsafe or talk about self-harm, stay on the line and contact local emergency services or a crisis hotline.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort.

If you’re able, pick up and:
- Listen more than you talk; validate their feelings (“I’m so sorry. I’m here with you.”).
- Ask gentle questions: “Do you want to talk about it?” “Is there anything you need right now?”
- Avoid fixing or clichés; just be present.
- If you truly can’t talk long, let them know you care, schedule a call soon, and check in the next day.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort, if you have the capacity.

How to handle the call:
- Start with simple empathy: “I’m so sorry. I’m here with you.”
- Let them talk. Listen more than you speak; don’t rush to fix or use clichés.
- Validate feelings: “It makes sense you feel this way.”
- Ask what they need right now: to talk, sit in silence, or help contacting someone.
- Offer practical support: help call a nearby friend/family member, arrange a ride, or stay on the line.
- If they mention wanting to harm themselves or not feeling safe, keep them on the phone and contact local emergency services or a crisis line.

If you truly can’t answer:
- Send a quick message: “I saw your call. I care and will call you at [time]. Are you safe right now?”
- Follow up as promised and check in again the next day.

Afterward, take a moment to decompress and set any needed boundaries for rest.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort.

Why: They’re grieving and reached out to you specifically. A few lost minutes of sleep is usually worth being there in a crisis.

How to help, briefly:
- Pick up, listen more than you talk, and validate (“I’m so sorry. I’m here with you.”).
- Avoid fixing or platitudes; ask what they need right now (to talk, sit in silence, practical help).
- If you truly can’t talk, send a quick text: “I’m here, can I call you in 10–15 minutes?” and follow through.
- If they mention wanting to harm themselves or seem in immediate danger, stay on the line and help them contact local emergency services or a crisis line.

b) Answer the call and offer comfort.

A brief, compassionate check-in can make a big difference during acute grief. Keep it simple:
- Start with empathy: “I’m so sorry. I’m here with you.”  
- Listen more than you talk; let them share memories and feelings.  
- Validate: “It’s completely okay to feel overwhelmed.”  
- Gently assess safety and support: “Are you alone? Is there someone with you?”  
- Offer a small, concrete next step: “I can stay on a bit and check in again tomorrow.”

If you truly can’t pick up, send a quick message: “I saw your call and I’m here for you. I can call in 10–15 minutes—do you want me to?” If they seem at risk of self-harm, contact local emergency services or a crisis line (e.g., in the U.S., 988).

Answer if you have the bandwidth to be present and helpful; if not, let it go to voicemail, then follow up promptly (a quick text now and a call first thing). If there’s any sign of immediate risk to their safety, contact local emergency services or a crisis line.
